channel.title: Game Director on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Game Director on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: My Ideas That Got Rejected
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my ideas that got rejected from my own games, and I’m going to start all the way back with Fallout, which was now what?

Almost 30 years ago that that project got started and I was Project Lead or design director or game director on a lot of these titles and my own ideas didn’t make it in. So I’ve done a lot of videos where I talk about learning from failure and I talk about how you can learn from other people’s failures. So learn from mine, learn from ideas I wanted to put into games that got thrown out. Let’s start with Fallout.

item.title: Definitions Are Hard (aka Why A Game Director Is Necessary)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how definitions are hard and I’m going to subtitle this — why game director is necessary.

What I’m going to talk about today is why it is so incredibly hard to define something, a term, a concept, an object. People frequently argue because — not because they believe different things, but because they’re using different definitions of words, of Concepts, and they’re kind of talking past each other, and this happens a lot in the game industry and some of you are probably going — it’s not hard to define a term. That’s how you start, you define your terms and then you move on from there. No, it is really hard to define something and people will disagree on the most basic, fundamental of definitions. So I want to tell you a story first before I dive into the game development parts of here. Really, Tim, tell a story, so the very first day of an AI class I had in college, the teacher posed a question to the class, and this was a 90-minute class and this is all we talked about.

item.title: Game Flavor
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game flavor, and I’m deliberately doing this on a Friday, Funday, because what I’m going to talk about is very subjective.

So kind of view: this, as a Tim talks about game development at another, very high level of description. Uh, and I almost didn’t do this video because what I mean by game flavor is really hard to describe. I’m going to try to do it, but it’s hard.

item.title: High Level Design Direction
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about highlevel design Direction.

This is something that the game director, creative director, whatever it’s called — on your project. This is probably the single most important task that the person in this role does. I’m going to talk about it, but it’s also something that I think is misunderstood or underestimated by a lot of people who aren’t in the role and by people who first step into the role. Um, and what I mean by highle Design direction is you basically have to watch everything going into the game, everything: system mechanics, story elements, quests, Maps, dialogue, NPC, the names of things, lore, text, everything — to make sure it all fits. And that highle — whatever view, whatever you know your take on — your vision of this game should be: all that stuff has to fit and serve that Vision.

item.title: Why I Directed One Last Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I directed one last time and specifically what I mean by that is why I stepped up to be a game director on The Outer Worlds, even after I said I wi it.

This is to answer a question from zombified gamer series who asks: I’m curious on what exactly happened that allowed you to step up and direct the The Outer Worlds. You mentioned that something happened and healed you over time. I’m genuinely curious if you could tell what that story on how you overcome, overcame the demoralization that you went through.

item.title: Ego And Game Directors
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about ego and game directors and how those two things often seem conflated in people’s minds and I want to talk about.

I’ve talked about how to be a good game director, but I’m going to talk specifically about what it is about having a strong ego, but not being egotistical means for being a good game: d dor. So the game director I’ve talked about is the person who holds the vision for the game being made. So questions that arise: does this feature belong? Is this character a good character? Is this storyline good? Ultimately, those go up to the game director. Now, usually they have a lot of leads, a lead narrative, a lead artist, a lead uh system mechanics person, and those people are deciding a lot of this, but they’re doing it based on the director’s philosophy, and being a good game director means having a very clear philosophy of game design and communicating it to the team.

item.title: How To Be A Good Game Director, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do a part two about being a game director, and I’ll put the part one Link in the description.

The reason I wanted to talk about this was not only after I did when I did the first one about being a game director. There’s some stuff I forgot to mention because it’s all — it’s an awfully vague term, enroll. But also people ask questions that I wanted to get to an answer, but most of them are fairly short answers. So I thought, okay, when I have enough of these, I’ll do a part two and talk about being a game director, part two. One thing I mentioned, uh, in that video and other ones, is that when I play games now, I am overly Critical with them. I, I’ll be playing a new game. It will install startup and I’m like, uh, why did? Why is this the Cinematic you chose? This isn’t teach me about your world.

item.title: Design Pillars
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design pillars.

This is something that I’ve mentioned several times in several past videos. I’ve seen a lot of comments from people asking me to explain them, kind of like what, are they, how are they used, how do you write them? So I thought I’d describe what they are and then, because they’ve changed over the years, I’m Gonna Give You examples of what fallouts were and then what arcanums were and finally what The Outer Worlds design pillars were, and you can kind of see for yourself, um, how they changed over the years and how they were used. So first of all, let me Define design pillars. They are the high level guidelines for making the whole game, every aspect, the Story, the system, mechanics, the — you know — ads, um, if you look at the pillars, you have a good idea of what kind of game is being made. Not just you know what the setting is and um, you know what the — the basic genre of the game is, but you have a good idea of what the intention is of this game — the - the pillars are the foundation upon which the entire game rests and because of that you have to make these early. These are foundational, if you’re making a game and two years in you decide to change design pillars, you’ve just changed the game and you have possibly invalidated all the work you just did in the last two years. So design pillars tend to be made as early as possible in the design process.

item.title: How To Be A Good Game Director
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what it’s like to be a game director now.

This is what it’s called now. It used to be called the project leader sometimes in the 90s. Producer meant the person who was really running the show or was the lead designer, but they’ve really Consolidated a lot of these roles now and game directors — what’s usually used as the person who creative director sometimes — is the person who’s running the project. It’s kind of like the showrunner on a TV show. Basically, this is the person who has the vision for the game being made. That doesn’t mean people on the team don’t share that vision, but if you think of it as a boat, there’s one person at the wheel who’s steering, and he’s usually either the captain or he’s taking his. The choices he’s making are coming from the captain. I don’t think you’d want to be on a boat where anybody could run up at any time.
